content:
Stanford’s 2026 AI Index confirmed what every CS grad has been quietly suspecting: the first rung of the ladder isn’t just smaller. It’s gone.

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I want to be careful with the framing here, because “AI is killing jobs” articles are usually either hysterical or hollow.
This one isn’t either. The numbers came out a few months ago, and most of the developer world looked away from them as fast as politely possible — because what they show isn’t a trend, isn’t a temporary correction, and isn’t going to reverse on its own.
Stanford’s 2026 AI Index landed earlier this year. Buried in the 400-page report, between benchmark scores and policy commentary, was one statistic that should have been the headline of every tech publication that week:
Employment for software developers aged 22 to 25 has dropped nearly 20% since 2022.
Not total developer employment. That’s roughly flat. The 20% drop is concentrated entirely at the youngest end of the profession — the developers who were supposed to become the next decade’s mid-level engineers, staff engineers, principal engineers. The exact people the industry needed to be hiring to keep the pipeline alive.
That’s the part nobody at the top wants to talk about. Here’s the rest of it.

The Numbers People Wish Weren’t Real
Pull the data together from the credible sources — Stanford, US Federal Reserve Bank of New York, industry hiring reports — and the picture sharpens fast.
CS graduates now face a 6.1% unemployment rate. Computer engineering grads, 7.5%. For context, art history sits at 3%. Civil engineering at 1%. Nursing at 1.4%. Computer science, the degree that was sold to millions of people for two straight decades as the most reliable economic ticket in modern America, is now producing graduates who are unemployed at roughly twice the rate of art history majors.
Entry-level developer postings dropped 67% between 2022 and 2026. In the UK, the same drop ran 46% in 2024 alone, with 53% projected by end of 2026. The share of juniors in new IT hires fell from 15% to 7% in the same window — meaning for every 100 new developers being hired today, where there used to be 15 juniors, there are now 7.
Q1 of 2026 alone saw 52,050 tech layoffs. AI was explicitly named as the cause in roughly 25% of those firings — not “macroeconomic conditions,” not “strategic realignment,” but AI in the official statement. Meta, Google, Amazon, Atlassian, and Salesforce all said the quiet part out loud.
And here’s the framing that crystallized it for me, taken from a hiring manager interviewed in CIO magazine:
“Why hire a junior for $90K when GitHub Copilot costs $10?”
That sentence is the entire story compressed into one question. Once a senior engineer asks it out loud, the answer in nearly every company has been the same.

What Specifically Got Replaced
Be precise about this, because “AI replaced juniors” is too vague to be useful.
The junior developer job, as it existed from roughly 1995 to 2023, was structured around a specific set of tasks: boilerplate code, simple CRUD endpoints, unit test scaffolding, basic documentation, small bug fixes, and the routine work that gave a new developer time to build judgment by doing the easy stuff first.
Every single one of those tasks now takes Claude Code, Cursor, or Copilot somewhere between seconds and minutes to complete, for $10 to $20 a month versus $85,000 to $140,000 a year. Companies report 40–55% more code output per sprint after adopting these tools. One senior engineer with Claude Code now ships what used to require a senior plus a junior. The economic logic of hiring the junior, for the work they were originally hired to do, collapsed.
The work didn’t disappear. The role built around that work did. And the developers who would have moved through that role to become tomorrow’s senior engineers are now competing for positions that don’t exist, against a tool that doesn’t sleep, ask for benefits, or need mentorship.

The Part That Should Genuinely Scare People
Here’s where this stops being a story about today and becomes a story about 2032.
Every senior engineer writing production code today was once a junior who needed someone to take a chance on them. The path runs: junior → mid-level → senior → staff → principal. Each rung depends on the rungs below it actually existing. You can’t grow into a senior engineer if the entry-level job that produces senior engineers has been demolished.
Industry analysts are already modeling what comes next. A projected 67% drop in junior hiring is expected to produce a 60–70% reduction in qualified tech leads and architects by 2032. The US is already on track to face a shortage of 1.2 million software engineers by 2026 — and that was before the junior pipeline collapsed further.
Addy Osmani, the LeadDev contributor who has written more honestly about this than almost anyone, put it bluntly: “Cutting off the talent pipeline entirely creates a leadership vacuum in 5–10 years.”
The companies enjoying 40–55% productivity gains today are setting up a hiring crisis in 2030 that will make 2021’s “we can’t find senior developers anywhere” look like a warm-up. The same executives signing layoff memos right now will be the ones panicking about senior engineer shortages in five years, paying $400K base salaries for the talent they refused to grow themselves.
That’s not speculation. That exact pattern played out after 2008. Companies froze junior hiring during the recession. Four years later, every team was struggling to find mid-level developers, because the pipeline had been cut at the bottom in 2009, and the consequence finally surfaced in 2013. It’s documented. It’s repeating. And almost nobody at the executive level is changing course this time, because the productivity gains in the current quarter are too good to refuse.

What the “AI Is Just a Tool” Crowd Is Missing
Every time this gets discussed, a particular response shows up: “AI is just a tool; it makes developers more productive, juniors just need to learn to use it.”
This is half right and dangerously incomplete.
Yes, AI raises productivity for everyone using it. Yes, juniors who learn to direct, evaluate, and extend AI-generated code have real value. None of that changes the basic economic math that companies are running, which is: if one senior with Claude Code ships what used to require two people, why hire the second person at all?
The “just learn to use AI” advice quietly assumes there are still entry-level positions to use those skills in. The Stanford data shows the entry-level positions themselves are what’s vanishing — not because juniors are bad at AI, but because the budget line for hiring juniors has been reallocated to AI tooling and senior salaries. You can’t AI-fluency your way into a job that no longer exists on the org chart.
The honest version of the advice would be: become exceptional at the small remaining slice of junior work that companies still hire for, primarily AI-assisted productivity from day one, deep specialization in regulated domains (fintech, healthcare, defense, climate tech), or contribution to the AI tooling ecosystem itself — which are exactly the niches where Anthropic, OpenAI, Cursor, Vercel, and Hugging Face are quietly accelerating junior hiring while everyone else freezes.
That’s a much narrower path than “learn JavaScript and apply to startups.” It’s the path that actually exists in 2026.
If you’re navigating this market and want 75 tested Claude prompts that genuinely accelerate your skills:
👉 The Claude Playbook →

What Happens From Here
This won’t reverse on its own. AI tools improve every quarter. Each generation closes more of the gap between “needs a human” and “Claude Code can handle it.” The set of tasks that justify hiring a junior at $90K continues to shrink, not because the juniors got worse, but because the floor of AI capability keeps rising.
A few things are likely from here. Bootcamps will continue collapsing, because the value proposition of “learn to code in 12 weeks and get a $90K job” no longer matches the market. CS enrollment will start declining within the next 2–3 years, as the gap between degree promise and graduate employment becomes too visible to ignore. Some non-trivial fraction of future principal engineers is currently switching majors to finance, medicine, or law — and the industry will never know what it lost.
The companies that genuinely understand the long-term consequence — IBM tripled its junior intake in early 2026 while the rest of the industry froze — are quietly positioning themselves to dominate the 2030s senior talent market. They’re paying short-term inefficiency to own the next generation of engineering leadership. Almost nobody else is doing this, which is precisely why the few who are will benefit so much.

The Honest Closing
If you’re a CS student, recent grad, or someone planning a coding career right now, none of this is meant to be a doom verdict on your future. It’s meant to be an honest read on what the actual market looks like in 2026, so you can plan against it instead of plans built on a 2019 version of the same industry.
The opportunities still exist. They’re narrower, more specialized, more domain-specific, more concentrated in AI-adjacent companies than they used to be — but they exist. Most of the surviving paths run through specialization that AI can’t credibly replicate yet: regulated domains, security work, system design, the parts of engineering where “wrong” carries real legal or human consequences.
What’s gone is the broad, easy onramp. The “learn to code, get any junior position, grow from there” path that worked for two decades is what specifically died. Pretending otherwise just sets up another wave of people for the same hard landing the 2024 and 2025 grads are already living through.
The senior developers reviewing this in five years will be reading it in a market with nowhere near enough of them. Whether the industry course-corrects before then or rides the productivity gains all the way into the talent shortage it’s currently engineering — that’s the open question. Nobody at the top wants to answer it on the record.

I write about Claude, AI tools, and what’s actually happening in this industry — honestly, not hyped — every week in my free newsletter.
